coredata.dc:title: High quantum-yield lignin fluorescence materials based on polymer confinement strategy and its application as a natural ratiometric pH sensor film
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuan
Shen, Qi
Li, Shengren
Xue, Yuyuan
coredata.dc:description: The high quantum-yield (QY) lignin fluorescence material at solid state was obtained via the polymer confinement strategy, using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as polymer matrix and organic solvent lignin (OL) as luminescent material. The absolute QY of lignin/PVA film could reach 13.68 % by optimizing the raw material of OL, of which was even higher than that in THF medium due to their hydrogen bond interaction for inhibiting aggregation and rigid environment for decreasing molecular rotation and vibration. In addition, under the encapsulation of PVA, the resistance of photobleaching of lignin fluorescence was also enhanced. Finally, OL was proved as a natural ratiometric fluorescence pH sensor from the electronegativity change of phenylpropane unit, and the fluorescence of lignin/PVA film showed a naked-eye recognition for spoiled pork detection. This work provided a new perspective to lignin fluorescence for really using as a feedstock for luminescent materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
High value utilization
Luminescent material
PVA
Food detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Lignin/PVA solution and films
Photobleaching of lignin THF solution and lignin/PVA film
Detection of ammonia water by lignin/PVA films
Optical property of OL from different lignocelluloses
Luminescence properties of coassembly lignin/PVA Films
Study of noncovalent interactions between lignin and PVA matrix
Ratiometric fluorescence pH response and the application for spoiled food detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work were supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908158 ) and the special fund for Science and Technology Innovation Teams of Shanxi Province (No. 202204051001009 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of OL in THF at 0.1 g/L, (B) FTIR spectra of OL powder, (C) QY of OL in THF at 0.01 g/L, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001. n.s., not significant, the P values were calculated by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey's correction, (D) Maximum fluorescence intensities of OL in different solvents at 0.1 g/L.

(A) Absorbance intensities of lignin/PVA films with different content of lignin, (B) Fluorescence spectra of films. Insets showed their corresponding photographs under 365 nm UV lamp, (C) Fluorescence intensities of films with different content of lignin, (D) Absolute QY spectra of films.

(A) Photographs of 0.1 g/L lignin water solution with different mass fraction of PVA at sunlight, (B) The corresponding scattered light intensity of 0.1 g/L lignin water solution with different mass fraction of PVA, (C) Fluorescence intensities and (D) lifetimes of 0.1 g/L lignin water solution with different mass fraction of PVA. Insets showed their corresponding fluorescence spectra.

(A) 1H NMR spectra of PVA (10 mg), lignin (5 mg), lignin (5 mg)/PVA (10 mg) in THF-d
8 (0.5 mL)/D2O (0.5 mL). (B) FT-IR spectra of pure PVA film, lignin powder and lignin/PVA film.
Ratiometric fluorescence pH response curve of cedar (A), poplar (B) and bagasse (C) lignin in aqueous solution at 0.1 g/L. Insets showed their corresponding fluorescence spectra.
Fluorescence spectra of lignin model compounds (assisted dissolution by THF) at different pH solution.

Fluorescence spectra of lignin/PVA films under NH3 atmosphere for 0–30 min, (A) Cedar lignin, (B) Poplar lignin, (C) Bagasse lignin. (D) Fluorescence spectra of bagasse lignin/PVA film before and after fumigation by NH3 (λEx=365 nm). Insets showed the photographs under 365 nm UV lamp.
Photographs of 4 g pork samples with different storage time for 1–7 days under (A) sunlight and (B) 365 nm UV lamp.

head.ce:title: High quantum-yield lignin fluorescence materials based on polymer confinement strategy and its application as a natural ratiometric pH sensor film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Computer Science and Technology, Taiyuan Normal University, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Computer Science and Technology, Taiyuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Computer Science and Technology, Taiyuan Normal University, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Taiyuan University of Technology, Shanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high quantum-yield (QY) lignin fluorescence material at solid state was obtained via the polymer confinement strategy, using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as polymer matrix and organic solvent lignin (OL) as luminescent material. The absolute QY of lignin/PVA film could reach 13.68 % by optimizing the raw material of OL, of which was even higher than that in THF medium due to their hydrogen bond interaction for inhibiting aggregation and rigid environment for decreasing molecular rotation and vibration. In addition, under the encapsulation of PVA, the resistance of photobleaching of lignin fluorescence was also enhanced. Finally, OL was proved as a natural ratiometric fluorescence pH sensor from the electronegativity change of phenylpropane unit, and the fluorescence of lignin/PVA film showed a naked-eye recognition for spoiled pork detection. This work provided a new perspective to lignin fluorescence for really using as a feedstock for luminescent materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin is a natural aromatic polymer, mostly consisted of coniferyl, sinapyl or p-coumaryl alcohol, and accounts for 15–25 % of lignocellulosic materials (Lin et al., 2022a, 2022b; Sun, 2020; Rinaldi et al., 2016). At present, most lignin is used as a low-grade fuel. Valuable lignin-based products, namely as value-added chemicals and construct functional materials, are explored to improve the economy of biorefining process (Chio et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2022a, 2022b). Therein, technical lignin has been directly used as a dispersant and a binder without laborious process due to its intrinsic properties of amphiphilicity and cross-linking reaction (Xiao et al., 2021; Shorey et al., 2021; Pang et al., 2021). Developing lignin-based functional materials from its unique physicochemical properties has become an important direction to promote lignin utilization. Comparing with cellulose and hemicellulose, lignin has a conjugated structure and is one of the most important autofluorescent molecules in plant (Donaldson, 2020), indicating that it has the potential to serve as an optical material. Primarily, lignin fluorescence has been used for the label-free detection of visualizing lignin microscopically in wood and paper fibers (Donaldson and Williams, 2018). Recently, the interest was also focused on the field of fluorescence material, like photoluminescence/phosphorescence materials, fluorescence sensor and tracer (Li et al., 2018; Yuan et al., 2021; Xue et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2022; Manigrasso et al., 2022).

Despite new studies emerged in endlessly, lignin was not commonly used as a feedstock for luminescence materials, since lignin fluorescence was always perceived as 1) undetermined and 2) low quantum yield (QY). According to previous researches, lignin fluorescence was mainly related to the chemical structure and aggregation state of sub-structural units (Shen et al., 2022; Auxenfans et al., 2017; Escamez et al., 2021), of which were controlled by the species, extraction method and external environment. With the change of chemical structure and environment, different electronic energy level structures of lignin were formed. For instance, Takada et al. reported that tunable light emission from lignin was achieved by adjusting the factors above or using different chemical treatments, resulting from the change of conjugative and reabsorption effects (Takada et al., 2022). In addition, lignin fluorescence showed obvious photobleaching phenomenon, which made its fluorescence more undetermined (Wang et al., 2016; Pandey and Vuorinen, 2008). On the other hand, the low QY of lignin was resulted from two crucial factors. One was that excessive intermolecular stacking of phenylpropane units induced aggregation-caused emission quenching (ACQ), especially in the solid state (Deng et al., 2011), and the other was energy transfers from lignin excited state to quenching or movement groups (Xue et al., 2016, 2020). For example, we recently reported that the QY of lignosulfonate could be increased by 35 times to about 12 % by hydrogenolysis to break the intrinsic aggregation and reduce content of reabsorbing groups in lignin (Shen et al., 2022). Similar strategy, obtaining small molecular fragments lignin for fluorescence enhancement, was also implemented by solvent extraction (Niu et al., 2017). Harsh conditions, like strong acid/alkali and solvothermal et al., could also converted lignin into strong fluorescence fragments with a size of less than 10 nm, namely as lignin carbon quantum dots (Xu et al., 2021). Although high QYs were achieved, these laborious processing decreased the attractiveness of lignin fluorescence. Solvent assemble could also enhance the QY of lignin by restricting its intramolecular motion, but this part of the non-radiative transition was very small (Xue et al., 2016, 2020). How to obtain the stable fluorescence property and appropriate QY of lignin for practical application facilely was the key to solving the problem.

One strategy to suppress the nonradiative transition was doping or copolymerizing planar organic molecules into rigid matrix, namely as polymer confinement (Wang et al., 2019). The doped molecules were well distributed and confined into the polymer matrix that can restricted molecular aggregation, rotation, and vibration (Wu et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2016). Furthermore, the polymer matrix could enhance the processability of fluorescence materials (Kim et al., 2019). Zhao and co-workers assembled carbazole-based luminescence molecules with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to afford high radiative transition (Wu et al., 2019). There were also some reports on lignin/polymer hybrid materials. Takada et al. mixed lignin into poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) to obtain transparent fluorescence films (Takada et al., 2022). Chen et al. used lignin/PVA composite film as the fluorescence sensor (Ma et al., 2018). We found that lignin fluorescence did not disappear like the solid powder of lignin when the traditional solvent medium was replaced by a polymer matrix. In the same time, we believed that the photostability of lignin might be enhanced by the encapsulation of polymer. Although these lignin/polymer systems were prepared, the effect and relationship between lignin fluorescence and polymer matrix were unclear.

Herein, the optical property of organic solvent lignin (OL) from hardwood, softwood, and grass lignocellulose respectively in tetrahydrofuran (THF) were analyzed firstly. The property of lignin fluorescence was mainly related to the source under the same extraction method. The QY of softwood OL was the highest, of which could reach 10.87 % from cedar, followed by hardwood lignin, and grass lignin was the lowest. All the lignin showed little QY in water or solid-state due to ACQ effect. Then, inspired by the polymer confinement strategy, PVA was used as the polymer matrix for OL to obtain high QY lignin material at solid state, of which was increased to 13.68 % from cedar. The change of fluorescence intensity and co-assembled behavior of lignin/PVA film were studied systematically. Finally, based on the natural ratiometric pH response of lignin, the application of spoiled food detection of lignin/PVA film was also explored.

All the wood fiber and chemicals were commercially sourced. Cyclohexane, toluene, dichloromethane, dioxane, glycol, HCl, NaOH and ammonia water were analytically pure. THF was chromatographic pure and used after double distillation. Other chemicals were used without further purification. PVA was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Its molecular weight was 44,000 Da, alcoholysis degree was 87.0–89.0 mol %, and viscosity was 80.0–110.0mPa·s. Distilled water was from laboratory filtration system.
The process was according to the literature (Shuai et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Lignin/PVA solution and films
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enhance the mixing effect of lignin and PVA, the precursor of colloidal sphere solution of lignin was prepared by alkali-acid treatment. OL was dissolved in alkali solution with pH at 12, and following neutralized by concentrated hydrochloric acid. Then, amount of PVA solid and lignin colloidal sphere solution were mixed to obtain lignin/PVA solution with different content of PVA (1 wt %, 2.5 wt %, 5 wt %, 7.5 wt %, 10 wt %). The mixing conditions of lignin/PVA solution was optimized by fluorescence spectrometer, as shown in Fig. S4. The lignin/PVA solution with stable fluorescence property was prepared at 90 °C and stirred for 3 h. Lignin/PVA films were prepared by coating 400 μL of lignin/PVA solution onto 2 × 2 cm quartz plates and drying at room temperature for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photobleaching of lignin THF solution and lignin/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Photobleaching tests were monitored by the time-scan mode of fluorescence spectrometer. A slit width of 10.0 nm; a photomultiplier voltage of 400 V and a scan time of 600 s were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ammonia water by lignin/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: 3 mL of 25 wt % ammonia water and lignin/PVA film were placed in a closed culture for 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30 min, respectively. The fluorescence changes of composite films were monitored by fluorescence spectrometer.

Lignin/PVA films were fumigated for half an hour in the enclosed plastic box, where pork sample was placed at room temperature for 1–7 days. After that, the photographs of films under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp respectively were captured by digital camera.

The UV–vis spectra of lignin solution were measured between 250 and 500 nm by UV-765 (Jingke, Shanghai). Fluorescence spectra were determined by Hitachi F-2700. Fluorescence mode: scan speed 300 nm/min; delay time 1.0 s; slit width 10.0 nm; photomultiplier voltage 400 V; response time 2.0 s. The quantum yield was measured using Edinburgh FLS1000 instrument by integrating sphere accessory. The excitation and emission wavelengths of OLs were shown in Table S1. Fluorescence lifetime was recorded by an Edinburgh FLS980 instrument with the excitation wavelength at 375 nm. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed on a ZetaPALS instrument from Brookhaven Co. A laser power of 40 mW, a light angle of 90°, a laser wavelength of 670 nm, a liquid parameter of water and a run duration of 1 min were used within all DLS measurements. DLS measurement repeated 10 times for one sample. The scattered light intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrometer with the light wavelength of 670 nm. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) was recorded using IRSpirit-T QATR-S instrument (Shimadzu Co., Japan) between 4000 and 400 cm−1 by attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory. 1H NMR spectra of samples were tested by DRX-400 spectrometer (400 MHz 1H NMR frequency, Bruker Co., Ettlingen, Germany). The solvent for 1H NMR measurement was THF-d 8/D2O mixture and its volume ratio was 1:1. THF-d 8 was selected as the deuterium solvent in the parameter setting. All tests were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property of OL from different lignocelluloses
ce:section.ce:para: According to the proportion of substructures, lignin could be divided into three categories, including hardwood lignin, containing the highest syringyl (S) content, softwood lignin, dominated by guaiacyl (G), and grass lignin, containing many hydroxylphengl (H) units (Neiva et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Two kinds of raw materials were selected from the three categories of lignocellulose above, respectively, namely poplar and birch, cedar and pine, bagasse and corncob. The optical properties of these OLs, extracted by dioxane system, were studied. The optical properties were collected in Table S1. The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of different kinds of OLs were shown in Fig. 1 A. It found that the same category of lignin always showed semblable characteristics. Grass lignin had the largest extinction coefficient (ε), reaching 28.30 L/g·cm at 280 nm. The ε at 280 nm of softwood lignin was slightly higher than that of hardwood, at about 19.48 L/g·cm. The difference in ε was mainly related to their chemical structure. The substructures of softwood and hardwood lignin were composed of G and S units connecting by β-O-4 ether bonds. However, grass lignin has many phenyl acrylic acid structures and ester bonds, resulting in a high proportion of hyperchrome, like CC and CO (Pongchaiphol et al., 2022). Except for 280 nm, another absorption peak at about 315 nm was recorded in grass lignin, which was consistent with the absorption spectrum of phenyl acrylic acid, as shown in Fig. S1. The different contents of CC and CO groups were confirmed by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 1B. 1711 and 1660 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration peaks of CO and conjugated CO, respectively (Chen et al., 2018). 1593, 1508, 1455 and 1391 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration peaks of conjugated CC bonds (Chen et al., 2018). Their corresponding absorption intensities of grass lignin were stronger than those in hardwood or softwood lignin. In terms of fluorescence, hardwood and softwood lignin had the similar excitation and emission peaks, located at about 316 nm and 380 nm, respectively. The conjugation of grass lignin was increase and the two peaks were red shifted to 330 nm and 425 nm, respectively. Among the three categories of OL, the fluorescence intensity of softwood lignin was the highest, followed by hardwood lignin, while grass lignin was the lowest. The QY of cedar, pine, poplar, birch, bassage and corncob lignin were further calculated, of which were 10.87 ± 0.44 %, 9.3 ± 0.35 %, 7.48 ± 0.36 %, 5.86 ± 0.15 %, 0.37 ± 0.03 % and 0.32 ± 0.02 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1C. These results were consistent with the intensities of UV absorption and fluorescence emission of OLs. The difference of QY between hardwood and softwood lignin might be related to the content of methoxy. Previous studies showed that as an electron donor group, methoxyl could activate phenol to quench the fluorescence (Shen et al., 2022). The quenching fluorescence of grass lignin might be related to the rich quenching groups, such as CO. Thus, a part of OL showed a high absolute QY, exceeded 10 % in THF, and had the potential to use as the feedstock for luminescent materials theoretically. However, as an amphiphilic polymer, lignin could be well dissolved in medium polar solvents to maintain a large QY but not in both low and high polar solvents. As shown in Fig. 1D, the fluorescence intensity of lignin increased first and then decreased with the increase of solvent polarity. Compared with the intensity in good solvent, like THF, lignin fluorescence intensity was decreased to about 1.12 % in water medium. The practical application of lignin fluorescence was seriously restricted by its ACQ effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence properties of coassembly lignin/PVA Films
ce:section.ce:para: The key to improving the QY of lignin in water or solid-state was breaking the intermolecular hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions to inhibit intrinsic aggregation (Zhao et al., 2017). The research of increasing lignin dissolution behavior had been reported. Singh et al. found that lignin had a high solid loading in ethylene glycol (>30 wt %), which was attributed to the strong hydrogen bond interactions between ethylene glycol and the free hydroxyl groups present in lignin (Sun et al., 2016). Wang et al. reported that the solubility of lignin was increased from 0 to 80 g/L in water by adding urea as the hydrogen bond donor (Wang et al., 2017). It was found that adding hydrogen bond donor was a feasible method to break the aggregation of lignin micelles. Since PVA had a large amount of hydroxyl groups, it could form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions with compounds containing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Zhang et al., 2019). We selected PVA as hydrogen bonding donor and polymer matrix to achieve high QY lignin fluorescence materials. Since the same category of lignin exhibited the similar emission, cedar, poplar and bagasse lignin were employed for subsequent studies. The optical properties of the films consisting of PVA and kinds of OL respectively were investigated. The doping films with different content of lignin were prepared to obtain the best coassembly system. When the lignin concentration changed from 0 to 0.5 g/L in the precursor solution, no visible phase separation was observed and the absorbance of lignin/PVA films at 280 nm showed a good linear relationship ( Fig. 2A). It indicated that OL and PVA had a good compatibility. Under the UV light of 365 nm, lignin/PVA film showed strong blue fluorescence. The emission came from lignin rather than PVA, because the pure PVA film has no fluorescence under the same condition (Fig. 2B). The emission peak of lignin/PVA film was blue-shifted by about 10–30 nm compared with that in THF solution. The optical properties were collected in Table S1. With the increase of lignin, the film fluorescence increased linearly at first and then stabilized, with an inflection point at about 0.4 g/L (Fig. 2C). Meanwhile, the concentration-induced fluorescence quenching of lignin/PVA film was weaker than that in THF solution (Fig. S2), further revealing the inhibiting effect of PVA for lignin aggregation. Then, the absolute QY of lignin/PVA films were recorded (Fig. 2D). The values of lignin/PVA films with cedar, poplar and bagasse lignin respectively were 13.68 %, 3.80 % and 0.43 %. Compared with their THF solution, the retention rate of QY of poplar lignin/PVA film was 50.80 %, surprisedly, the QY of lignin/PVA film with cedar and bagasse was even increased to 1.26 and 1.16 times, respectively. As such, a high fluorescence efficiency of lignin fluorescence material at solid state was achieved successfully by the PVA polymer strategy. It suggested that this phenomenon was ascribed to the noncovalent interactions between lignin and PVA matrix in the doping film, affording hydrogen bond donor for inhibiting aggregation and rigid environment for decreasing molecular rotation and vibration. Meanwhile, PVA showed different inhibition effects on the non-radiative transition of lignin from various sources. We proposed that due to the differences in the chemical structure of the three category of lignin, the noncovalent interactions forces between lignin and PVA must be different, resulting in their different fluorescence behaviors. Furthermore, except for the enhancement of fluorescence intensity, encapsulating lignin in the polymer matrix could enhance the stability of lignin fluorescence (Fig. S3). Lignin fluorescence showed strong photobleaching phenomenon, which was an important factor to its indetermination property. The cedar and poplar lignin fluorescence decayed rapidly under UV radiation in THF. After 10 min irradiation, only 20–30 % of the initial intensity remained. On the contrary, the retention rate of lignin fluorescence intensity in PVA film was increase to about 70–80 % under the same condition. It suggested that the photo oxidation reaction, the reason for lignin photobleaching in THF, could be effectively avoided by PVA encapsulation. The fluorescence intensity of bagasse lignin in THF or film medium showed a slight increase with the radiation of UV light. It might be related with the destruction of fluorescence quenching groups in grass lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of noncovalent interactions between lignin and PVA matrix
ce:section.ce:para: For revealing the coassembly process, the research of solution behavior of lignin/PVA mixed aqueous solution was needed. Cedar lignin was employed for subsequent studies. Cedar lignin existed as colloidal spheres in aqueous solution by hydrophobic interaction. DLS showed that the average diameter of lignin nanoparticle was about 805.90 nm and a typical Tyndall effect was observed in the photograph, as shown in Fig. 3A. With the addition of PVA, the mixed solution became transparent and its scattering light intensity was gradually decreased (Fig. 3B). The result indicated that PVA alleviated the aggregation behavior of lignin in water solution. The decrease of lignin nanoparticle size did not directly recorded by DLS, because the viscosity and refraction of mixed solution was influenced by the addition of high mass fraction of PVA. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of lignin/PVA mixed solution sufficiently certified the solution behavior above. The fluorescence spectra and emission intensities of 0.1 g/L lignin aqueous solution with different PVA content were shown in Fig. 3C. In the water medium, the fluorescence intensity of lignin was insignificant and a red-shift emission peak appeared at 410 nm due to ACQ effect. With the addition of PVA, the emission intensity was gradually recovered. When the PVA content increased to 10 wt %, the retention rate of lignin fluorescence intensity was reached 70.24 %, compared with that in THF solution. To understand the result above, the transient fluorescence was further recorded (Fig. 3D). The fluorescence lifetime of lignin in THF could reach 2.4 ns, while it decreased sharply to 0.8 ns in aqueous solution. After adding PVA, the fluorescence lifetime of lignin was increased to about 2 ns. It revealed that the non-radiative transition of lignin fluorescence enhanced in water, while its radiative transition was partially restored by PVA as inhibiting the aggregation of lignin.

The coassembly process was also demonstrated by 1H NMR spectra in D2O and THF-d 8 mixed solution ( Fig. 4A). The proton peak of hydroxyl group of PVA appeared at 3.99 ppm and showed 0.012 ppm shift to low field after complexing with lignin. At the same time, the methoxyl hydrogens from lignin also showed 0.013 ppm shift to low field. However, the aromatic hydrogens from lignin only showed 0.006 ppm shift. The proton shifts proved the existence of the intermolecular forces between PVA and lignin (Xu et al., 2021), and the methoxyl group in the substructures of lignin was more affected. With the weakness of intramellar aggregation, the electron shielding effect for lignin proton decreased, resulting in the low-field shift in 1H NMR. Moreover, the formation of coassembly between lignin and PVA was further confirmed by FT-IR (Fig. 4B). The vibrational peak of hydroxyl group of lignin was recorded at 3425 cm−1, and a largely shifted was observed after doping into PVA film. Furthermore, the Me-O vibration peak of lignin also exhibited a small shift from 1266 cm−1 to 1260 cm−1. Based on the NMR and FT-IR studies, the assembly mechanism could be proposed. When a large amount of PVA polymer was added, the original lignin nanoparticle could be dissolved due to the hydrogen bonding interaction between hydroxyl groups in PVA and hydrophilic polar groups (hydroxy and carboxyl functional groups etc.) in lignin (Zhang et al., 2018). PVA chain could isolate the substructure of lignin to avoid the unfavorable aggregation, resulting in the recovery of lignin fluorescence. Moreover, the coassembly could effectively suppress the aggregation, vibration and rotation of lignin fluorophore, especially in the film state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence pH response and the application for spoiled food detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As a natural fluorescence material, lignin had the potential as a ratiometric fluorescence probe. In the study of cell wall imaging, researchers found that the cell wall fluorescence changed from blue to green during acid-base transformation due to the ionization effect of grass lignin (Lichtenthaler and Schweiger, 1998). Then, we also previously reported that the emission peak of lignosulfonate shifted from 400 nm to 466 nm when pH was changed from 7.50 to 12.86, however, the fluorescence red shift of alkali lignin was not recorded (Xue et al., 2020). If OL itself could be used as the ratiometric fluorescence pH probe, the attractiveness of lignin fluorescence would be improved. The fluorescence spectra of colloidal sphere solution of cedar, poplar, and bagasse lignin respectively at different pH were studied, as shown in Fig. 5(A–C). At neutral pH, the emission peaks of the three lignin were located at 360, 365, and 400 nm, respectively. With the increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity at the original wavelength was decreased, while a new emission peak appeared at the long wavelength. The stokes shift of the three lignin reached 70, 70, and 50 nm, respectively. At the same time, all the three lignin showed ratiometric fluorescence response at alkaline pH (cedar I
430
/I
360 , poplar I
430
/I
360 , bagasse I
450
/I
400 ). The pKa of cedar, poplar, and bagasse lignin were calculated by 11.20, 10.50, and 10.20, respectively.

Combining with the pKa value and chemical structure of lignin, it was supposed that the ratiometric response was derived from the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl. For revealing the mechanism, there β-O-4 lignin model dimers with different functional groups, including phenethoxybenzene, 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethan-1-ol, and 4-(1-hydroxy-2-phenoxyethyl)phenol, were selected for fluorescence study. As shown in Fig. 6, the maximum emission peaks of three β-O-4 lignin model dimers were all around 300 nm in solution. As the solution pH changed from acidic to basic, the fluorescence intensity of non-phenolic lignin models was firstly increased and then decreased, whereas no new peak was appeared at long wavelength. Nevertheless, the phenolic lignin model showed a new emission peak at 400 nm along with the increase of solution pH. It indicated that the pH-induced red-shift of lignin fluorescence was attributed to the electronegativity change of phenylpropane unit. We continued to study the fluorescence behavior of ferulic acid, a specific substructure from grass lignin. The results showed that the fluorescence behavior of ferulic acid at different solution pH was almost consistent with that of bagasse lignin.

The fluorescence response of lignin/PVA films was next studied. However, after soaking in NaOH solution with different pH value, the serious crimping deformation was formed in lignin/PVA film due to the poor water resistance of PVA matrix. We believed that the deformation of lignin/PVA film could be avoided by fumigating with alkaline gas. Combined with the condition in our laboratory, the method of NH3 fumigation was used, as shown in Fig. 7. After fumigation by NH3, the expectative peaks at long wavelength of doping films with cedar and poplar lignin respectively were not observed. With the increase of fumigation time, the fluorescence intensity was decreased and the stokes shift was only about 20 nm. On the contrary, the response behavior of the film containing bagasse lignin was the same as the solution state. With the increase of fumigation time under NH3 atmosphere, the emission peak of bagasse lignin/PVA film was shifted from 405 nm to 440 nm and the fluorescence intensity increased by 2.62 times. When the excitation light was switched from 329 nm to 365 nm, the increase time of fluorescence intensity could be reach 4.61-fold, realizing the naked-eye recognition, as shown in Fig. 7D. By comparing the pKa values of the three lignin and NH3, we suggested that NH3 did not effectively ionize the phenolic hydroxyl in cedar and poplar lignin due to their relatively large pKa value. According to the naked-eye recognition of bagasse lignin/PVA film for NH3, the application for spoiled food detection was displayed, of which the bioamine was formed during protein denaturation. As shown in Figs. 8A, 8B and S5, the bagasse lignin/PVA films were fumigated for half an hour in the enclosed plastic box, where an amount of pork sample was placed at room temperature for 1–7 days. With the increase of storage time, the color of pork changed from light pink to light yellow due to the increase of spoilage. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of the film increased significantly with the increase of storage time under 365 nm UV light, which mainly resulted from the increase of bioamine content. The amount of pork samples could also affect the sensitivity of the naked-eye recognition. Compared with the result in Fig. 8, when the amount of pork samples changed from 4 g to 1 g, the sensitivity of the naked-eye recognition decreased, as shown in Fig. S5. This phenomenon might be related with the concentration of bioamine. When the due to the concentration of bioamine. The result reveled that grass lignin/PVA film could be used to detect food detection. Meanwhile, it should be emphasized that due to the poor water resistance of PVA matrix, high moisture would cause serious crimping deformation in lignin/PVA film and generate interference for fluorescence detection. This phenomenon might result from the change of hydrogen bonding interactions between PVA and lignin. The present device could only be used in qualitative determination for alkaline media.

Our work analyzed the fluorescence property of OL from different biomass sources and then provided a general method to prepare high QY lignin fluorescence material by polymer confinement strategy. We found that the fluorescence efficiency of natural lignin was dependent on its substructure and chemical environment. Owing to the low content of quenched groups, the QY of softwood lignin was higher than that of hardwood and grass lignin, and its absolute QY was more than 10 % in good solvent. Under the polymer confinement effect of PVA, the substructure of lignin was well distributed and confined into the polymer matrix to achieve high QY at solid state by restricting molecular aggregation, rotation, and vibration. The QY of cedar lignin/PVA film was up to 13.68 %, even larger than that in solvent medium. In addition, under the encapsulation of PVA, the resistance of photobleaching of lignin was also increased from 20 % to 30 % to 70–80 %. These properties provided new insights and experimental evidence for lignin fluorescence to using as the feedstock for fluorescence materials. We believe that the performance could be further enhanced by optimizing the structure of lignin and polymer. Finally, the application of spoiled food detection was displayed based on the natural ratiometric pH response of lignin. It demonstrated the naked-eye recognition of spoiled pork by lignin/PVA film.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Yuyuan Xue reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work were supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908158) and the special fund for Science and Technology Innovation Teams of Shanxi Province (No. 202204051001009).

ce:section.ce:para: Support information includes fluorescence and UV spectrum of phenyl acrylic acid, fluorescence intensity of different concentrations of lignin in THF, fluorescence intensity of PVA/lignin solution under different mixing conditions, photobleaching Data of lignin in THF solution or film.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2023.116384.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
